LinkedIn: all-in-one integration hypothesis — solo founder story

There are roughly 53 million family caregivers in the US. Most of them have zero medical training.

They're managing medication schedules for aging parents, tracking lab results they barely understand, and trying to coordinate between multiple doctors who don't always talk to each other.

I think about this a lot while building TrackMe+. The healthcare system assumes patients manage their own care, but the reality is that millions of family members are doing it instead. And the tools available to them are basically... a pill organizer and a notebook.

That's a gap worth closing.

When I designed TrackMe+, the caregiver use case was always front of mind. A Family Plan so you can set up a dedicated account for the person you're caring for. Medication schedules organized by time of day. Lab results you can actually trend over time instead of losing in a stack of papers.

It's not about replacing medical professionals. It's about giving caregivers a fighting chance at keeping everything organized.

If you know a family caregiver, send them something useful this week. They probably won't ask for help themselves.

#HealthTech #Caregiving #DigitalHealth

I've talked to a lot of caregivers while building TrackMe+. Here's what I keep hearing:

They don't want another app that sends 47 notifications a day. They want something that just works quietly in the background and keeps things organized.

The features they actually care about might surprise you. It's not AI-powered anything or voice-activated whatever. It's:

Can I see all of Mom's medications in one place?
Can I track when she took her morning dose?
Can I look up her last lab results without digging through a folder?

Simple stuff. Executed well.

The health tech industry loves to build for the "empowered patient" persona. But a huge chunk of actual users are family members who didn't choose this role and are learning as they go.

Building for caregivers means building for simplicity, reliability, and peace of mind. Not feature bloat.

That's the lens I try to keep at TrackMe+. Every feature has to pass the test: would this actually help someone managing their parent's medications at 6am before their own workday starts?

#HealthTech #CaregiverTech #ProductDesign
